threats.title: Generation Increment Permanently Reverts Due to onlyOwner Modifier
threats.hypothesis_description: The protocol will permanently brick after 10,000 tokens are minted because the generation increment function attempts to call an external function restricted to the contract owner.
threats.investigation_rationale: TraitForgeNft._incrementGeneration() calls entropyGenerator.initializeAlphaIndices(). However, initializeAlphaIndices is restricted by the onlyOwner modifier. Since the deployment script does not transfer ownership of EntropyGenerator to TraitForgeNft, this call will always revert, permanently halting all minting after Generation 1.
deployment_assumptions: EntropyGenerator ownership is not transferred to TraitForgeNft after deployment
threats.config_evidence: deployTasks.ts lines 34-43 show addresses being set, but no call to entropyGenerator.transferOwnership(nft.address)

proof_of_signal.pattern: onlyOwner modifier on function called by another contract

preconditions: 10,000 tokens minted in the current generation
TraitForgeNft is not the owner of EntropyGenerator

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Normal protocol operation triggers the revert naturally
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit, but causes total loss of future protocol revenue and functionality

economic_questions: Can the protocol survive if stuck on Generation 1?
Are there any alternative ways to increment the generation?
validation_requirements: Verify ownership state of EntropyGenerator post-deployment
Test minting the 10,000th token to confirm revert

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Check if any other function can bypass this

threats.title: Public Entropy Batch Initialization Allows Total Outcome Control
threats.hypothesis_description: Publicly accessible entropy generation functions allow an attacker to manipulate the randomness seed for all future tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: The functions writeEntropyBatch1, writeEntropyBatch2, and writeEntropyBatch3 are public and use block.number to generate entropy. An attacker can pre-calculate which future block numbers will produce the most favorable entropy values (e.g., multiple 999999s) and execute these batch functions exactly in those blocks, gaining complete control over token traits.

proof_of_signal.pattern: public visibility on state-initializing functions using block.number

preconditions: Entropy batches have not yet been fully initialized

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker calls initialization functions at calculated block numbers
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker ensures high-value entropies are generated, then mints them to drain NukeFund

economic_questions: How many high-value entropies can be packed into a single batch?
What is the expected value extraction from controlling the entropy sequence?
validation_requirements: Simulate block.number brute-forcing to find optimal blocks
Verify lack of access control on batch functions

preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Multi-block planning, single block execution per batch

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately after deployment before legitimate initialization

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All legitimate minters and NukeFund participants

threats.title: Predictable Entropy Sequence Enables Sniping of High-Value Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: The exact traits of the next minted token are publicly readable, allowing attackers to snipe the most valuable tokens without needing to front-run.
threats.investigation_rationale: Entropy values are pre-generated. The getPublicEntropy function allows anyone to read the exact entropy value that will be assigned to the next minted token. An attacker can monitor the global token counter and submit a mint transaction exactly when a highly favorable entropy (e.g., 999999) is next in line.

proof_of_signal.pattern: public view function exposes future pseudo-random values

preconditions: Attacker monitors the current token ID count

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker waits for normal users to consume low-value entropies, then mints the high-value ones
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Sniping the 999999 entropy token allows claiming ~25% of the NukeFund

economic_questions: How often do high-value entropies appear in the sequence?
What is the ROI of sniping vs random minting?
validation_requirements: Calculate the exact NukeFund claim value for the top 1% of entropies

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to 25% of NukeFund per sniped token

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When currentSlotIndex points to a high-value entropy

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify getPublicEntropy matches actual minted entropy

threats.title: mintWithBudget Permanently Broken After Generation 1
threats.hypothesis_description: The batch minting function becomes permanently unusable after the first generation sells out due to an incorrect loop condition.
threats.investigation_rationale: mintWithBudget uses `_tokenIds < maxTokensPerGen` as its loop condition. `_tokenIds` is a global counter across all generations, while `maxTokensPerGen` is 10,000. Once Generation 1 completes, `_tokenIds` will be >= 10,000. For all subsequent generations, the loop condition will immediately evaluate to false, refunding the user's ETH and minting 0 tokens.

proof_of_signal.pattern: while loop condition uses global counter instead of generation counter

preconditions: Generation 1 has sold out (10,000 tokens minted)

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Natural protocol progression breaks the function
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit, but severely degrades user experience and protocol revenue velocity

economic_questions: How much does the loss of batch minting affect protocol revenue?
Will users pay the higher gas costs to mint one-by-one?

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Wasted gas on failed batch mints

threats.title: Predictable 98% Price Drop on Generation Increment Creates Arbitrage
threats.hypothesis_description: The minting price resets to near-zero when a new generation starts, creating a massive arbitrage opportunity for users who snipe the first tokens of a new generation.
threats.investigation_rationale: calculateMintPrice uses `startPrice + priceIncrement * generationMintCounts[currentGeneration]`. When a generation ends, generationMintCounts resets to 0. This causes the mint price to instantly drop from its peak (e.g., ~0.25 ETH for token 10,000) back down to ~0.005 ETH for token 10,001. Attackers can wait for the generation to roll over and snipe tokens at a 98% discount compared to the previous minter.

proof_of_signal.pattern: price calculation resets multiplier to 0 on generation increment

preconditions: A generation is about to reach its 10,000 token limit

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker waits for generation rollover to mint at base price
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker acquires Gen 2 tokens for 0.005 ETH while late Gen 1 buyers paid 0.25 ETH, allowing immediate profitable secondary market dumping

economic_questions: Does the market value Gen 2 tokens similarly to Gen 1?
What is the exact price delta at the boundary?
validation_requirements: Calculate exact price of token 10,000 vs 10,001

threats.title: Off-by-One Error Allows Entities to Exceed Forge Potential
threats.hypothesis_description: An off-by-one logic error allows every forger entity to breed one more time than its intended maximum limit.
threats.investigation_rationale: EntityForging.listForForging checks `forgingCounts[tokenId] <= forgePotential`. If an entity has a forgePotential of 1, it can be listed when its count is 0, forged (count becomes 1), and then listed again because 1 <= 1 evaluates to true. It can then be forged a second time (count becomes 2). This allows every forger to exceed its genetic limit by exactly one.

proof_of_signal.pattern: inclusive <= comparison allows off-by-one limit bypass

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker lists token when count equals potential, allowing one final forge
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker extracts one extra forging fee per entity they own, inflating supply

economic_questions: How much extra supply does this introduce to the ecosystem?
What is the total excess fee extraction possible?
validation_requirements: Test forging a token with potential 1 twice in the same year

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: One extra forging fee per owned forger
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Can be executed anytime limit is reached

threats.title: Uninitialized ageMultiplier Nullifies Hold-to-Earn Mechanics
threats.hypothesis_description: Because ageMultiplier is never initialized, token age contributes zero to the nuke factor, allowing immediate maximum payouts.
threats.investigation_rationale: The NukeFund contract calculates token age using `ageMultiplier`. However, `ageMultiplier` defaults to 0 and is never set in the deployment script. Consequently, `calculateAge` will always return 0, and the `finalNukeFactor` will rely entirely on the token's entropy. This destroys the hold-to-earn mechanic, allowing users to immediately nuke high-entropy tokens for maximum payouts without waiting for them to mature.
deployment_assumptions: Deployer does not manually call setAgeMultplier after running the deployment script
threats.config_evidence: deployTasks.ts does not contain any call to nukeFund.setAgeMultplier()
preconditions: Token has passed the minimumDaysHeld threshold (3 days)

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Users nuke immediately after 3 days instead of holding for months
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Users extract maximum value instantly, draining the fund faster than intended

economic_questions: How fast can the fund be drained without the age requirement?
Does this completely break the game theory of the protocol?
validation_requirements: Verify ageMultiplier state on testnet deployment

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Immediate realization of full entropy value

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Fund drained before their tokens mature

threats.title: Entropy Sequence Repeats Exactly After 10,010 Mints
threats.hypothesis_description: The pseudo-random entropy sequence loops back to the beginning after 10,010 mints, making all future generations completely predictable.
threats.investigation_rationale: EntropyGenerator generates exactly 10,010 entropy values (770 slots * 13 numbers). When currentSlotIndex reaches the end, it wraps back to 0. Because the entropy slots are never re-written after the initial batches, the exact same sequence of 10,010 entropies will be assigned to tokens in Generation 2 and beyond. Attackers who map the sequence in Gen 1 will know the exact traits of every token in future generations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: index wrapping without state regeneration causes sequence repeat

preconditions: More than 10,010 tokens minted across all generations

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: currentSlotIndex wraps to 0 without rewriting entropySlots
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker records Gen 1 entropies and uses them as a perfect map for Gen 2+
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker perfectly times mints to capture all high-value tokens in subsequent generations

economic_questions: Does the alpha index initialization alter the sequence enough to hide this?
How easily can a bot map and snipe the repeating sequence?
validation_requirements: Simulate 10,015 mints and compare entropies of token 1 vs token 10,011

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Zero chance of minting rare tokens
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Value of all rare traits in future generations

threats.title: High Entropy Tokens Can Drain ~25% of the Nuke Fund Instantly
threats.hypothesis_description: The unbounded initial nuke factor calculation allows a single high-entropy token to claim a disproportionately massive share of the Nuke Fund.
threats.investigation_rationale: calculateNukeFactor sets the initial nuke factor to `entropy / 40`. For the maximum entropy of 999999, this is 24999. The claimAmount is calculated as `fund * finalNukeFactor / MAX_DENOMINATOR` (100000). This allows a single token to claim ~25% of the entire Nuke Fund immediately after the minimum holding period. An attacker sniping high-entropy tokens can rapidly drain the protocol's treasury.

proof_of_signal.pattern: unbounded initial factor allows disproportionate fund claims

preconditions: Attacker possesses a token with entropy near 999999

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker snipes max entropy token and nukes it
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker extracts 25% of the fund, leaving little for other participants

economic_questions: Is it intended that one token can drain 25% of the fund?
What happens if multiple 999999 tokens are nuked sequentially?
validation_requirements: Calculate exact payout for 999999 entropy token
Model fund depletion over 5 sequential high-entropy nukes

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost to acquire high entropy token
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Massive ROI if fund is large

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When NukeFund is at peak value

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 25% reduction in their potential nuke value
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Up to 50% of NukeFund across a few tokens

threats.title: forgeWithListed Does Not Refund Excess ETH
threats.hypothesis_description: Users who accidentally send more ETH than the required forging fee will have their excess funds permanently trapped in the contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: The forgeWithListed function in EntityForging requires `msg.value >= forgingFee`. However, it does not refund any excess ETH sent by the caller. The exact forgingFee is split between the NukeFund and the forger owner, but any amount above forgingFee remains permanently locked in the EntityForging contract, as there is no withdrawal function.

proof_of_signal.pattern: missing refund logic for excess msg.value

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: require(msg.value >= forgingFee) without refunding msg.value - forgingFee
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: User error or frontend bug causes overpayment
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No attacker profit, but permanent loss of user funds

economic_questions: Can the trapped ETH be rescued by the admin?
How likely are users to overpay?
validation_requirements: Verify absence of sweep/withdraw functions in EntityForging

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: User frustration and loss of funds

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Check all ETH routing paths in function

threats.title: buyNFT Does Not Verify Listing is Active
threats.hypothesis_description: The trading contract fails to check the isActive boolean on listings, relying entirely on the seller address not being zero.
threats.investigation_rationale: The buyNFT function in EntityTrading checks `listing.seller != address(0)` but fails to check `listing.isActive`. While cancelling a listing currently deletes the struct (setting seller to address(0)), if any future logic modifies a listing without deleting it (e.g., pausing a listing), buyers could still purchase it. This is a defense-in-depth failure.
preconditions: A listing exists where seller != address(0) but isActive == false

initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Buyer acquires an NFT that was intended to be off-market

economic_questions: Is there any current code path that sets isActive to false without clearing the seller?
Could a reentrancy attack create this state?
validation_requirements: Review all state transitions for the Listing struct

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Loss of NFT at potentially stale price

threats.title: Setting taxCut to 0 Causes Division by Zero, Halting Protocol
threats.hypothesis_description: If the admin sets the taxCut parameter to 0, all core economic functions will revert due to division-by-zero errors.
threats.investigation_rationale: The NukeFund and EntityForging contracts use `taxCut` as a divisor to calculate developer and protocol fees (e.g., `msg.value / taxCut`). If the admin accidentally or maliciously sets `taxCut` to 0 via `setTaxCut`, any transaction that routes ETH to these contracts (minting, trading, forging) will revert. This would completely halt the protocol's core economic engine.

proof_of_signal.pattern: division by configurable state variable without zero check

economic_questions: Is there any input validation on setTaxCut?
How quickly can the admin recover the protocol if this happens?
